Article.ArticleTitle: Unraveling synergistic radical and non-radical pathways in bromide and iodide-activated peroxymonosulfate systems for selective abatement of micropollutants.

Abstract.AbstractText: Halide ions, commonly found in industrial wastewaters, offer a promising pathway for peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation, yet the reactive species and their oxidation selectivity remain insufficiently understood. Herein, PMS activation by Br- and I- was systematically investigated for micropollutant degradation. Under typical conditions ([PMS]0 = 1 mM), PMS/0.005 mM Br- achieved >99 % removal of 1 μM BPA within 30 min at pH 3.0 via reactive bromine radicals and free bromine (HOBr/Br2), whereas PMS/0.02 mM I- showed a pH optimum at 6.0 (>99 % within 5 min) by reactive iodine species and free iodine (I2/I3-). Both systems preferentially degrade electron-rich micropollutants, such as bisphenol A (BPA), while compounds with strong electron-withdrawing substituents showed limited elimination. The degradation behavior of sixteen structurally diverse micropollutants highlighted the dominant roles of RBS in PMS/Br- and RIS/I3- in PMS/I-. Transformation products of BPA were identified by Q-TOF-MS/LC, and their model-predicted toxicity revealed potential secondary environmental risks. Real industrial wastewater was used as a reaction matrix, demonstrating that residual halide ions can effectively activate PMS for micropollutant removal despite complex matrix interferences. This study reveals the mechanisms of a catalyst- and energy-free halide-mediated PMS activation process, which holds potential economic and operational advantages compared with conventional PMS activation processes. However, a critical finding is the generation of toxic byproducts, highlighting that managing secondary pollution is essential for the technology's viable implementation.
